text: Latamar: Lost Mine to Curse of Strahd
Matthew Brown
Start Date: 3/28/23
Table of Contents
Quests Open Quests:
Major: Get Revenge:
Description:

Jove was murdered. It is Lysandra's sole goal and responsibility to avenge her fallen lover. The Black Spider is responsible for his death, and she will not rest until he has been killed. Further, The Black Spider also killed Gundren, a mentor to all, and he must be avenged. The Forge of Spells still has not been breached.
Reward:
Justice.
Explore the Forge of Spells Description:

The Forge of Spells still has not been fully explored. Nezznar still has not breached the forge. There are still things lurking in there. If the party is to free the forge, then a resistance front will form, allowing for rebels to gather against Strahd and Nezznar.
Reward:
The magic items hidden within the Forge.

Find Gina Description:
Reward:

Reward:
Closed Quests: Major:
Find Gundren: Resolution: Gundren was found dead.
Clear out the Redbrands:
Resolution: The Redbrands were run out of Montera.
Minor:
Deliver Supplies to Montera:
Resolution: The supplies were delivered.
Banshee's Bargain:

See: Shrine of Luck Description: Deliver a gift to the banshee, and she may grant the party two questions. Resolution: The party got their two questions (who owns the spellbook and where is the Black Spider), then stole back their offerings and left.
Deliver Gundren's Body

Description : Gundren, brutally murdered, requested to be transported to Solario where his estate is. Resolution: The party transported him from WEC to Solario, and left his body with Brother Ivan to be buried and allowed to rest in peace.
Locations in The Sapphire Coast:
End of Wave Echo Cave (Statue Room):

Wave Echo Cave located here. Description: 'As you all find yourselves out of the dream which Jove gifted you with, sleep beyond that point is … difficult, to say the least. The stone room around you is cold, though well decorated and fairly well stocked. As you all try to sleep, Haus you are explicitly reminded of Jove being dragged around the corner, of the pain on his unconscious face, of the fear you felt when the doppelganger stole his face. The rest, you are haunted by Pedro, of his screams as he was mauled by wolves and goblins, of all the blood. You do manage a long rest, though it is hardly restful. and fairly lost. The room held overnight, though at some point the statue slid back to cover

'As you all begin to stir, your tired muscles aching and the cracking of your necks and backs, as you stretch to embrace the day, echo off the stone walls. You all feel cold, sterile, the secret passage, the riddle still carved into the inscription at its foot.
'My friends, two fewer than yesterday, what do you wish to do?'
Cave Exit:

Description: 'As you exit the cave, in the hazy light of morning, the clearing before you appears entirely unremarkable. As you step into the scarce grass that covers the ground, the dew wets the bottoms of your pant legs, and the air is bitter cold against your cheeks. The most prevalent smell of the clearing is that of blood, and as you survey, you notice a small figure hunched against a tree on the outskirts of the clearing. 'The broad shoulders, prevalent beard, and now-dull glint in his eyes reveals this to be none other than Gundren (see: Gundren Rockseeker). He clasps at his abdomen where two large incisions ooze puss, blood, and green ooze. His body seems almost as though it were discarded like trash amongst the weeds. 'With his dying wish, he breathes: 'Please, bring me to Solario.'' 'Beside him, you find a note that details a Lord who calls himself Strahd von Zarovich asking if the Black Spider may attempt to prove their worth to rule under the vampire.
'To the Black Spider,
Your recent activities have not gone unnoticed. It appears you seek power and control in this land, much like I do. I have an offer for you, a chance to prove your worth.
Meet me on the road. Be willing to produce a sign of your commitment to our cause. If you can impress me, you shall find yourself in a position of influence and authority in this realm.
-Strahd von Zarovich''
NPCs: Gundren Rockseeker
-Dwarvish, bearded, bright blue eyes
-Asks the party to deliver his body to Solario

-Gundren Rockseeker is a stoic and distinguished dwarf whose presence commands respect. His appearance is a testament to his dwarvish heritage and unwavering determination. With a sturdy build and broad shoulders, Gundren exudes a sense of reliability and resilience.

-Gundren's beard, a hallmark of dwarven pride, is thick and well-kempt, reaching down to his chest. Its deep mahogany color contrasts strikingly with his vibrant and bright blue eyes, which sparkle with an intense and unwavering spirit.

-Dressed in practical yet finely crafted dwarvish attire, Gundren carries himself with an air of authority and gravitas. His clothing and accessories are adorned with intricate designs and symbols that signify his dedication to his clan and his love for his homeland.

-Despite his formidable appearance, Gundren's gaze carries a warmth that contrasts with his beard's sternness. He speaks with a deep, resonant voice that commands attention, and he has an unwavering sense of duty.

-Upon meeting the party, Gundren beseeches them with his dying wish: to transport his body to Solario, a testament to his commitment to his people and his faith in the party's abilities. His character reflects the dwarven values of honor, loyalty, and a deep connection to his homeland.
The Main Road/Triboar Trail:

Description: As you follow the tracks that lead to the main road, you find yourself on a desolate path flanked by dense, eerie forests on both sides. The fog shrouds the surroundings in a ghostly embrace, making it difficult to see more than a few feet ahead.

The ground beneath your feet is uneven, and the tracks of carts and the deep imprints of horse hooves suggest recent activity. The eerie nature of the place is overwhelming. Everything is quiet, save for the occasional rustling of leaves and the distant hoot of an owl. The whole world appears to be holding its breath in anticipation.
Bats with beady, crimson eyes watch you from the gnarled branches of the ancient trees that line the road, their presence enhancing the unsettling atmosphere.

The smell of damp earth and rotting leaves mingles with the acrid tang of something unnatural in the air. It's a scent that sends a shiver down your spine, making you keenly aware of the otherworldly forces at play in this fog-clad land.

Every step you take on the cobblestone road seems to echo, the sound reverberating through the eerie stillness. You feel as though unseen eyes are upon you, and the silence is broken only by the occasional mournful hoot of an owl, as if nature itself is warning you to proceed with caution.
Montera:

Description: (old) The frontier town of Montera is built on the ruins of a much older settlement. Hundreds of years ago, the old Montera was a thriving human town whose people were firmly allied with the dwarves and gnomes of the Mondelver's Pact. However, the same orc horde that sacked the mines at Wave Echo Cave laid waste to the settlement, and Montera was abandoned for centuries.

In the last three or four years, hardy settlers from the cities of Solario have begun the hard work of reclaiming the ruins of Montera. A bustling frontier settlement has grown up on the site of the old town, and is home now to farmers, woodcutters, fur traders, and prospectors drawn by stories of gold and platinum in the foothills of the Sword Mountains. Unfortunately, more than a few bandits and brigands have settled here as well, taking advantage of the fact that the area has no local lord or authority to chase them off. A gang known as the Redbrands has controlled Montera for the past two months, extorting and bullying everyone in town. The gang is led by a mysterious figure known to the townsfolk as Glasstaff.

Description 11/7/23: Same Montera as before: Montera, a humble farming village, comes into view as you approach. It's nestled in a valley surrounded by rolling hills and fields of crops. The eerie nature of the land seems to have spared this peaceful hamlet, but a sense of quiet foreboding still lingers in the air.

The village consists of a cluster of quaint cottages and farmhouses with thatched roofs, their walls adorned with vibrant wildflowers. Small, well-tended gardens and orchards dot the landscape, offering a stark contrast to the foreboding world beyond.

As you enter the village, the cobbled streets are worn and uneven, suggesting a long history. The residents, mostly farmers, go about their daily tasks with a weary yet determined air. They give you curious, albeit guarded, looks, as if they can sense the mysterious aura surrounding your group.

A central square features a well, its waters crystal clear, and a stone statue of a forgotten hero who seems to cast a watchful eye over the village. The village's simple chapel stands nearby, its presence a reminder of faith amidst the encroaching darkness.
The village inn, known as "The Stonehill Inn," stands at one end of the square, its windows glowing warmly as the innkeeper (see: Elsa Stonehill) bustles about, preparing for the evening's guests.
Despite the eerie ambiance of the land, Montera retains a sense of rustic charm and tranquility, serving as an oasis of normalcy in the midst of the unknown.
Sublocations: Stonehill Inn

Description (old): In the center of town of Montera stands a large, newly built roadhouse of fieldstone and rough-hewn timbers. The common room is filled with locals nursing mugs of ale or cider, all of them eyeing you with curiosity.

Behind the bar there is a medium-height, large-framed, artificially redheaded Barmaid named Elsa, who smiles a broad, welcoming smile at you all when you come in. The tables are about half empty, rough hewn wooden and half the chairs around these tables are old kegs. The air smells sweet and it's warm in here, the air warms your faces and you're all suddenly aware of the weight and thickness of your armors or clothes.
Rumors
-Syf: a hunter
-By Narth, an old farmer: "Sister Garaele, who oversees the Shrine of Luck, recently left town for a few days, then returned wounded and exhausted." See: Shrine of Luck
-By Elsa, a gossipy barmaid: "Daran Edermath, the orchard keeper, is a former adventurer." See: Edermath Orchard
-By Lanar, a miner: "Orc raiders have been seen on the east end of Triboar Trail. The townmaster (Harbin Wester) is looking for someone to run them off." See: Townmaster's Hall

-By Trilena, the innkeeper (Toblen Stonehill)'s wife: "Thel Dendrar, a local woodcarver, stood up to the Redbrands a tenday ago when they came by his shop and leered at his wife. The ruffians murdered him. Several townsfolk saw it happen. The Redbrands grabbed his body, and now his wife, daughter, and son have gone missing too."
-By Pip, Toblen Stonehill's young son: "Qelline Alderleaf's son Carp said he found a secret tunnel in the woods, but Redbrands almost caught him." See: Phandalin Miner's Exchange
-Any NPC at the inn knows the Redbrands are trouble and frequent the Sleeping Giant tap hose
The Sleeping Giant

Description (old): The Sleeping Giant is a ramshackle taproom at the east end of town. Four human ruffians linger on the covered porch, perched on empty ale barrels or leaning against the wall. They all wear grimy scarlet cloaks, their sullen stares fixed on you as you approach.
One of the thugs spits on the ground. Well, well," he snarls. "Here's a whole pack of little puppies. What do you want, puppies? Come here to bark at us?"
They all wear grimy red cloaks and their hands are on their weapons.
Alderleaf Farm
On the outskirts of town, a small, red-roofed house rests at the foot of a large crop field.
The farmer, a wise halfling woman of 45, Qelline Alderleaf is pragmatic and kind.
-The party may stay in her hayloft if they wish, free of charge
-She presents the party with the quest: Reidoth the Druid
-Reidoth knows the entirety of the Sapphire Coast
-Ruins where she dwells are 50 miles NW of Montera
-Just west of Solaia Wood
-Ruins of Thundertree
Barthen's Provisions

A large, red, wooden building rests on the northeast of Montera. The structure looks as though it may have been built on top of a pre-existing foundation, where this newer building sits like a free-hand circle. Around the side of the building, you see two young Thistle)

human lads lifting crates off from a wagon and into the trading post. (They are Andar and The inside of the trading post is fairly well stocked. You speculate that you could find most any adventuring goods here, or at least any that are fairly cheap. Elmar Barthen is the human shopkeep, he is lean and balding, maybe fifty years old or so, but has a kind smile.
"Well howdy there fellas, what can I do you for today?"
-he views Gundren Rockseeker as a friend, and was excited about talks of the Lost Mine and the Mondelver's Pact in the nearby hills
-Gundren Rockseeker has two brothers: Nundro Rockseeker and Tharden Rockseeker, who are camped somewhere outside of town
-They should return "Oh, any day here now"
-Business is tough bc of the Redbrands which shake local businesses down and flout the townmaster, Harbin Wester's authority
-Redbrands frequent the Sleeping Giant
Edermath Orchard
A tidy little college rests on the outskirts of Montera beside an apple orchard. See: Daran Edermath.
Lionshield Coster
"Hanging above the front door of this modest trading post is a sign shaped like a wooden shield with a blue painted lion on it."
-Slightly smaller than Barthen's Provsions
-Owned by the Lionshields
-A merchant company based in Yatar, over 100 miles east
-Hit hard by banditry
-Most recent Lionshield caravan never arrived
-The master of this post is 35 year-old Linene Graywind who is tall and lithe, with dark hair and dark eyes. (sharp-tongued)
-Knows bandits have hit caravans and taken goods, though doesn't know who is responsible
-goods are in Cragmaw Hideout
-Linene has weapons and armor for sale
-won't sell to Redbrands
-avoid the Sleeping Giant, Redbrands are trouble
-won't sell to dangerous folk
Montera Miner's Exchange

On the south side of town, there is almost a little shack of sorts. The front is open mostly, with a desk of sorts resting on the inside of the building. Behind the desk, a well dressed, stout human woman, with her hair in a tight knot on top of her head, sits with her feet kicked up on the desk. You notice that there are large file cabinets and a store room behind her. A sign sits on the front of the desk that says "GOODS FOR GOLD"
Her name is Halia Thornton (ambitious and calculating)
-She takes goods for gold, but she is stingy
-acts as a merchant and governing authority for Montera
-is a member of the Zhentarim
-she asks the party to handle the Redbrands at the Sleeping Giant
-offers 100gp to eliminate the leader who the ruffians call Glasstaff
-(she wants to take over the operation herself (DC15 insight to discern ulterior motive))
-if they slay the Redbrands, she privately approaches some of the party to join the Zhentarim, and if she does they receive the title of Fang
Shrine of Luck
-Temple to Fortunus
-Small shrine of stones taken from nearby ruins
-Under the care of Sister Garaele, who is a zealous young elf who wishes Montera to be rid of Redbrands
-She is a member of the Harpers
-Only reports on the happenings around Montera
-She offers the party the quest: The Banshee's Bargain
-her superiors asked her to go to Agatha to answer a question about a spellbook, but the creature did not appear for her
-She asks the party to bring Agatha a gift, a jeweled silver comb, and persuade the creature to tell what she knows about the location of a spellbook belonging to a legendary mage named Bowgentle
-She believes that if the party flatters Agatha's vanity, they may be able to trade the comb for an answer
-Offers the party 3 potions of healing for payment for their efforts
-She may approach members of the party, ask them to join the Harpers, and bestow upon them the rank of Watcher
Townmaster's Hall

"The townmaster's hall has sturdy stone walls, a pitched wooden roof, and a bell tower at the back. Posted on a board next to the front door is a notice written in Common. It reads: 'REWARD-Orcs near Wyvern Tor! Those of a mind to face the orc menace should inquire within.' The notice bears the town's seal and an indecipherable signature."
-Townsfolk elect townmaster each year
-Serves as a judge in minor disputes
-Keeps all records
-Current townmaster is Harbin Wester
-Old, fat, and pompous
-Intimidated by the Redbrands
-insists they are "just a merc guild, and not all that much trouble, really"
-hall has a jail in the cellar
-he carries the keys with him
-Offers the Orc Trouble quest
-head east along the Triboar Trail, bunch of orcs near Wyvern Tor
-offers 100gp to take care of Orc problem
Tresendar Manor

More broken down castle than a house, Tresendar Manor stands stalwart on the edge of town, on a low hillside amid woods and thickets. The manor is abandoned, but there are rumors of evil stirring in its belly.
NPCs of Montera:
Elsa Stonehill:
-Pretty, artificial red hair

-Gives the party a quest: Elsa pleads with the party to help her find her missing sister who was last seen near Solario. She believes her sister may have ventured into the fog-covered lands, and Elsa is desperate for any information that can lead to her sister's whereabouts.
Alaric, the Village Historian:
-Wizened old man with a collection of ancient scrolls and tomes

-Informs the party about the region's history and the legends surrounding the fog and the mysterious lord known as Strahd. Alaric hints at an ancient prophecy that may hold the key to sealing away Strahd.
Caleb Grimwood, the Coffin Maker:

-A tall, thin man with a perpetual stoop, Caleb Grimwood is the skilled and somber coffin maker of Montera. His bony fingers are stained with the rich hues of various wood finishes, and his eyes carry a deep, almost mournful, wisdom.

-Caleb's workshop is tucked away in the quieter part of the village, where the echo of hammering and the creak of wood blends seamlessly with the eerie stillness that envelops Montera. The shelves of his workshop are lined with various-sized coffins, each meticulously crafted with an unwavering dedication to his craft.
-Caleb is a man of few words, and his presence is marked by a solemn air of respect for the dead. He takes great pride in creating exquisite, yet somewhat eerie, coffins,
which have become a source of local fascination. Some say his coffins hold special qualities, while others whisper about the strange symbols he carves into each one.

-While not directly involved in the town's daily activities, Caleb is a repository of local knowledge, including tales of the eerie nature surrounding Montera and the legends of Strahd. He is willing to share these stories with those who show respect for the mysteries that shroud their land. Caleb may be the key to unlocking hidden knowledge or clues related to the overarching narrative of your campaign.
Daran Edermath
-Daran Edermath is a silver-haired, half-elven elf of well over 100 years. He was a fighter in the Kraken Coast, far to the southeast, and returned to his home of the Solario region after retirement.
-Owns Edermath Orchard
-He is a member of the Order of the Gauntlet, though is no longer active.
-What does he know:
-He's concerned about the Redbrands
-would like someone to teach them a lesson
-main Redbrands safe house is under the Tresendar Manor (this is the Redbrand Hideout)
-He has heard stories from prospectors from hills northeast of Montera that someone is digging around the ruins at Old Owl Well.
-These prospectors have also told tales of being chased away by undead.
-Asks characters to go to the ruins, its a couple days march outside of town (Northeast)
-The ruins are an old watchtower of an ancient magical empire names Netheril and he worries that dangerous magic may be dormant there
-Joining the Order of the Gauntlet:
-After the Old Owl Trouble quest, he will approach members of the party privately, and if they agree to uphold the virtues, he grants them the title of Chevall
Solario:
Description 11/7/23:

As you crest the outlying hills, the fog begins to thin, revealing the sprawling silhouette of Solario, a once-thriving coastal town. The eerie nature of the fog-coated land is pronounced here, where the very edge of the world has receded into sheer cliffs that plummet into the roiling sea below.

The distant silhouette of Solario emerges from the gray mists, its once-bustling harbor and waterfront obscured by the shroud of fog. The town's towering walls stand as solemn sentinels, guarding what remains of this isolated town from the encroaching darkness.

The atmosphere is heavy with silence, and even from this distance, you can sense the watchful eyes of the town's inhabitants, who peer out from windows and crevices, ever vigilant in these uncertain times.
Sublocations
Solario Harbor:

Once a bustling hub of trade, Solario Harbor now lies eerily quiet, shrouded in mist. Abandoned ships, their masts tattered and sails in disarray, bob on the still waters. The eerie, unbroken silence is broken only by the mournful calls of distant seagulls.
Cliffside Overlook:

At the town's edge, the cliffs offer a breathtaking but ominous view of the endless sea below. The mist clings to the cliffs, almost like ghostly fingers reaching for the precipice. Ancient stone markers stand at the brink, bearing enigmatic inscriptions.x
The Whispering Plaza:

Solario's central square, the Whispering Plaza, is encircled by once-grand buildings, their facades weathered and timeworn. A statue of a forgotten hero stands in the center, now worn and indistinct. Villagers gather here to exchange hushed whispers and wary glances.
-The party winessed Viktor's brother be blinded and killed here
St. Seraphine's Cathedral:

Solario's grand cathedral, dedicated to the goddess of light, stands at the heart of the town. Its stained glass windows have been shattered, and the once-glorious altar is in disrepair. The eerie silence within is palpable as you explore the sacred space.
Windows have been smashed, Brother Ivan is the only priest there, he is a Monk. (See: Brother Ivan)
Shadowmarket Bazaar:

Solario's bustling market square, now shrouded in an eerie quiet. The market stalls, once filled with exotic wares and aromatic spices, are empty and forlorn. The only sound is the soft rustling of the fog as it swirls through the abandoned stalls.
Eternal Grove Cemetery:

The cemetery in Solario could be known as the "Eternal Grove Cemetery." This name evokes a sense of peaceful, eternal rest and reflects the eerie nature of the town as a whole. The Eternal Grove Cemetery could be a solemn and haunting place, with ancient, gnarled trees and shadowy gravestones enveloped in fog.
The Rockseeker Estate:

Description 11/7/23: The Estate stands as a testament to the dwarves' craftsmanship and their deep connection to the earth. Nestled amidst rolling hills and expansive fields, the estate is an imposing sight. The property is surrounded by a formidable stone wall, built with precision and adorned with dwarven runes of protection. A grand, wrought-iron gate provides entry to the estate, flanked by tall stone pillars that bear the crest of the Dwurinhold family, an intricate anvil and hammer intertwined with the roots of a mountain. The gate is typically guarded by vigilant dwarven sentinels. The main residence is a sprawling stone mansion, constructed with ornate architectural details. Its thick walls and stout stone pillars reflect the dwarves' commitment to both aesthetics and security. Stained glass windows depict scenes from dwarven history and tales of their mining endeavors. The mansion's interior is decorated with rich wooden furnishings, tapestries that recount dwarven legends, and intricate metalwork. A grand hearth dominates the central hall, where a roaring fire warms the space, and a collection of dwarf-crafted masterpieces, from weapons to fine jewelry, is displayed with pride. The estate also features lush gardens and a meticulously maintained orchard, where fruit trees heavy with produce flourish. A network of well-tended paths leads to a serene courtyard with a stone fountain at its center. The courtyard offers a place for contemplation and reflection.
The dwarves' wealth is evident in the sprawling workshop and forges on the estate, where skilled artisans craft works of art and utility. The echoes of hammers striking metal
reverberate through the air.
Rockseeker Estate is not just a residence; it's a symbol of dwarven culture, showcasing their rich history, craft, and connection to the land. The estate is known for its open
hospitality, welcoming travelers and dignitaries who come to appreciate dwarven craftsmanship and forge lasting relationships.
See: Thoren, Gundren
The Silverwater Inn:
Description 11/7/23:
The Silverwater Inn is a well-established and inviting establishment nestled within the heart of Solario. It stands as a beacon of warmth and comfort against the backdrop of the
eerie, fog-clad town.
The inn's exterior is built with stone and timber, exuding an old-world charm. A sign bearing the image of a silver crescent moon and a cliff overlooks the entrance, providing an
indication of the inn's name.
As you enter, a rush of warmth and the soothing aroma of hearth-baked bread and hearty stews greet your senses. The main common area is adorned with wooden beams and cozy
nooks, each furnished with plush seating and low tables for patrons to relax and converse.
The central hearth, with a crackling fire, radiates a comforting glow throughout the room.
The walls are adorned with tapestries and artwork that hint at the inn's history and
Solario's past glory.
The inn's bar is a solid, polished wooden structure, and behind it, a skilled bartender pours drinks and concocts elixirs to satisfy all tastes. Shelves stocked with a variety of beverages
are showcased in the background.
Upstairs, well-maintained rooms offer a peaceful retreat for weary travelers. Each room is cozy and elegantly furnished with comfortable beds and soft linens, creating an inviting
atmosphere for a restful night's sleep.

The inn is known for its jovial atmosphere, live music performances by local bards, and a menu that includes regional delicacies and a selection of fine wines and ales. A common room provides a place for guests to unwind, share stories, or play games.
The Silverwater Inn is a sanctuary in the midst of Solario's eerie surroundings, where guests find refuge from the oppressive rule of Strahd and the enigmatic fog that blankets the town.
The party initially met Captain Seraphina here. They played Baldur's Bones.
See: Captain Seraphina, Gilda Oakheart
Solario NPCs:
Kael, the Mysterious Guide:
-Shrouded in a dark cloak with a hidden face
-Offers to guide the party through the perilous fog-covered lands. Kael hints at a way to confront Strahd and the Black Spider, but they demand a valuable item as payment for their guidance.
Mayor Alden:
-Portly, jolly man known for his hospitality
-Informs the party about the oppressive rule of Strahd, and shares rumors of Strahd's obsession with controlling the Forge of Spells.
Leora, the Apothecary:
-Elderly woman with a vast knowledge of herbs and potions
-Offers to provide the party with supplies and elixirs in exchange for rare and mystical ingredients found in the fog-covered lands.
-Shriveled, crazy voice, covered in robes
-Sold the party Elflume Herb
Viktor Lockewood, the Rogue Artisan:
-Scrappy, street-smart craftsman
-Shares knowledge of secret pathways and hidden treasures within Solario, encouraging the party to explore the town's underbelly and evade Strahd's watchful eye.
-Viktor is a wiry and scrappy man with a shock of unruly black hair that seems to defy any attempts to tame it. His piercing emerald green eyes flicker with intelligence and a mischievous spark.

-He stands slightly below average height but carries himself with a confident swagger. Viktor's nimble fingers are always in motion, whether he's tinkering with his latest invention or gesturing animatedly as he speaks.

-His clothing is an eclectic mix of patched leather and cloth, adorned with pouches, belts, and a variety of tools and gadgets. A wide assortment of trinkets and knick-knacks dangle from his attire, creating a cacophony of sounds as he moves.

-Viktor's scruffy beard is peppered with streaks of gray, hinting at his age and years of experience as a rogue artisan. His tan complexion and the faint lines etched on his face tell the story of a life spent exploring and surviving in the unknown.

-Despite his slightly disheveled appearance, there's an undeniable charm to Viktor that makes him approachable and endearing. His eyes sparkle with a playful wit, and he often wears a crooked grin that invites others to share in his sense of adventure.
-Viktor's brother was blinded and stabbed by Strahd for speaking out about wanting to go to his homeland (Eldronia)

-Eldronia is a realm shrouded in legends of ancient ruins, uncharted wilderness, and a rich tapestry of cultures and traditions. Viktor's wanderlust and rogue artisan skills were honed through years of traveling and exploring the untamed landscapes of Eldronia. His background in this unique land can provide a wealth of stories and experiences to enrich your campaign's narrative.
Brother Ivan, Priest of Light:
-Kind, gentle man of faith
-Sleight slavic accent

-Brother Ivan is a gentle and kind man of faith, known for his calming presence and warm disposition. He has a slight but sturdy frame, with a posture that reflects his years of dedication to the church.

-His graying hair, neatly combed, is touched with silver at the temples, and he keeps his beard well-trimmed. Brother Ivan's eyes are a serene shade of blue, with an expression that radiates compassion and wisdom.

-He wears a simple yet immaculate set of priestly robes, adorned with symbols of the goddess of light. The robes are pure white, symbolizing purity and holiness, and are marked by subtle embroidery that signifies his devotion to his faith.
-Brother Ivan's hands, weathered by years of prayer and service, convey a sense of strength and grace. They often bear the scent of incense and sacred oils used in his rituals.
-He carries a holy symbol, an intricately crafted amulet, around his neck. It glistens with a soft, ethereal light, a testament to the divine connection he maintains with the goddess of light.
-Provides the party with information about Strahd's vulnerabilities and weaknesses, suggesting that they seek holy relics to aid in their quest to seal him away.
Captain Seraphina:
-Stern, middle-aged woman with a no-nonsense attitude
-Reveals that Solario has been under the control of Lord Strahd von Zarovich, a powerful and enigmatic vampire lord. She implores the party to be cautious and secretive in their dealings.
Gina Ironheart: (Elsa's lost sister)

-Gina is a free-spirited nomad with a wild and untamed spirit. She shares a striking family resemblance with her sister Elsa, with fiery red hair and emerald green eyes, although Gina's hair is often adorned with feathers and beads, reflecting her nomadic lifestyle.

-Gina's attire is a patchwork of colorful fabrics, animal skins, and mystical trinkets, each representing her journey and the various cultures she has encountered. She carries a weathered leather satchel filled with herbs, curios, and tokens from her travels.

-Gina is known for her deep connection with nature and her ability to communicate with animals. She has a deep reverence for the land and its mysteries, which she shares with those who cross her path. Her insights into the natural world often lead her to unusual discoveries.

-While Gina has chosen a nomadic life, she remains deeply attached to her sister Elsa and is always eager to reunite with her. Elsa's plea to the party to find her missing sister Gina initiates a quest to seek out this elusive and enigmatic nomad. Gina may hold vital information about the strange occurrences in the fog-covered lands and the quest to seal away Strahd.
Thorn Ironforge, the Solarion Blacksmith:

-Thorn Ironforge is a burly and skilled blacksmith who stands as a pillar of strength in Solario. His impressive build is complemented by a full, well-groomed beard that seems to have absorbed the soot and heat of his forge. His dark eyes, often smudged with sweat and grime, reflect a steadfast determination.

-Thorn's workshop is a robust and organized space filled with a variety of anvils, hammers, and blazing furnaces. The rhythmic clang of his hammer striking metal resonates throughout Solario, an enduring testament to his dedication.

-Known for his craftsmanship, Thorn is adept at creating weapons and armor that combine both functionality and aesthetic appeal. He often adds intricate details to his creations, making them highly sought after by adventurers and soldiers alike.

-Thorn is a no-nonsense man, direct in his words and actions, with a strong sense of loyalty to Solario. He is respected in the town not only for his skill but also for his unwavering commitment to the town's defense. He is always eager to assist those who require his blacksmithing expertise, providing essential equipment for those brave enough to venture into the fog-covered lands.

-As a member of Solario, Thorn may share valuable information about the town's struggles under Strahd's rule and how the party can prepare for the challenges that lie ahead. His forge becomes a central hub for adventurers seeking to arm themselves against the encroaching darkness.
Thoren Stoneguard (Rockseeker's Keeper)

-Thoren Stoneguard is a middle-aged human who has worked as the steward and caretaker of the estate belonging to the three dwarves (Rockseekers) for many years. He stands tall and sturdy with a weathered face, a testament to his years of dedicated service.

-Thoren's attire consists of practical clothing suitable for outdoor work, such as a brown tunic, sturdy leather boots, and a wide-brimmed hat that shields him from the elements. A tool belt is always at his side, equipped with various items for estate maintenance.

-He carries himself with an air of reliability and a deep respect for the dwarves he serves. Thoren's eyes are kind and crinkle at the corners when he smiles. His graying hair and beard, neatly trimmed, accentuate his experienced and dependable demeanor.

-Thoren has a vast knowledge of the estate's history, architecture, and the many unique dwarven customs that shape its operations. He plays a pivotal role in ensuring the estate runs smoothly and is always ready to offer assistance or information to those who visit the property.
-Straight talking, but very appreciative
Gilda Oakheart the Tavernkeeper:
-Gilda Stoneheart is the warm and welcoming barkeep of The Silvercliff Inn. She is a middle-aged woman with a hearty laugh and a generous smile that instantly puts patrons at ease.
-Gilda's auburn hair is tied in a neat bun, and her hazel eyes sparkle with a vivacious spirit. She wears a white apron over a forest-green dress, reflecting her practical and friendly demeanor.

-She's known for her excellent memory and ability to remember the preferred drinks and stories of her regular patrons. Her laughter is infectious, and she often joins in on the merriment, creating a lively and convivial atmosphere.

-Behind the bar, Gilda tends to the myriad bottles and kegs, serving a range of fine wines, ales, and specialty elixirs. She's also skilled in crafting unique cocktails and beverages that incorporate herbs and ingredients from the surrounding region.

-Gilda is a source of local knowledge, stories, and rumors, making her a popular figure for those seeking information about the town, the fog, and recent events. She's discreet and values the privacy of her patrons, making The Silvercliff Inn a place where people can share their secrets and concerns.
-She's passionate about fostering community and unity in the midst of adversity and the rule of Strahd, and she often hosts events and gatherings in the inn to bring people together.

The fog spills out of the forest to swallow up the road behind you. Ahead, jutting from the impenetrable woods on both sides of the road, are high stone buttresses looming gray in the fog. Huge iron gates hang on the stonework. Dew clings with cold tenacity to the rusted bars. Two headless statues of armed guardians flank the gate, their heads now lying among the weeds at their feet. They greet you only with silence.

Towering trees, whose tops are lost in heavy gray mist, block out all but a death-gray light. The tree trunks are unnaturally close to one another, and the woods have the silence of a forgotten grave, yet exude the feeling of an unvoiced scream.

E. Village of Barovia
Approaching the Village:

Tall shapes loom out of the dense fog that surrounds ev erything. T he muddy ground underfoot gives way to slick, wet cobblestones. The tall shapes become recognizable as village dwellings. The windows of each house stare out from pools of blackness. No sound cuts the silence except for mournful sobbing that echoes through the streets from a distance.
House Occupants
RATS
A house infested with rats looks abandoned. The rats are servants of Strahd and attack if the characters explore the interior of the house.
BAROVIAN VILLAGERS

A house of Barovian villagers is home to ld4 adults (LG male and female human commoners) and ld8 - 1 children (LG male and female noncombatants). Characters who listen at the door hear low, muffled whispers from within. These villagers aren't interested in speaking with strangers and never attack first, always fleeing from danger if possible. At night they cower by candle light and keep makeshift holy symbols close at hand.
STRAHD ZOMBIES

When the characters open a door or a shuttered window of a house infested with Strahd zombies, they are assailed by the stench of death. If the characters dare to enter, the zombies converge on their location.
Sublocations:
Bildrath's Mercantile
The sparse light from this building spills out from behind drawn heavy curtains. A sign over the door, creaking on its hinges, reads "Bildrath's Mercantile."

The establishment is 70 feet long by 40 feet wide. The owner, Bildrath Cantemir (LN male human commoner), sells items from the Adventuring Gear table in the Player's Handbook, but only items with a price lower than 25 gp in the table, and he sells them for ten times the price.

Bildrath trades with the Vistani when they pass through. He is also happy to make a profit from any strangers unlucky enough to find themselves here. He serves his own interests and offers no sanctuary. He never bargains since, as he says, "If you want it badly enough, you'll pay for it." He has no competition in the village.

If the characters give Bildrath a hard time, he calls Parriwimple (LG male human), his nephew and stock boy, to help him out. Parriwimple has the statistics of a gladiator, except that he has an Intelligence of 6 and doesn't carry a shield (AC 14). Parriwimple's real name is Parpol Cantemir, but no one in the village calls him that. His muscles rippling beneath his leather tunic should give ample notice of his strength. At the same time, Parriwimple is simple-minded. He is devoted to his uncle and will not follow the characters as long as Bildrath has something to say about it.
Blood of the Vine Tavern

A single shaft of light thrusts illumination into the main square, its brightness looking like a solid pillar in the heavy fog. Above the gaping doorway, a sign hangs pre cariously askew, proclaiming this to be the Blood on the Vine tavern.
The tavern building is about 60 feet square. Close inspection of the sign reveals that it originally read "Blood of the Vine." (An "n" has been scratched over with the "f.")
This once finely appointed tavern has grown shoddy over the years.
A blazing fire in the hearth gives scant warmth to the few huddled souls within. They include
the barkeep,
three Vistani sitting together,
and a man named Ismark Kolyanovich-who happens to be the son of the village burgomaster, Kolyan lndirovich.
Mad Mary's Townhouse
A moaning sob floats through the still, gray streets, col oring your thoughts with sadness. The sounds flow from a dark, two-story townhouse.

The house, which is about 40 feet square, is boarded up and barricaded from the inside. Mad Mary (CN female human commoner) sits in the center of the floor in an upstairs bedroom, clutching a malformed doll.
She is lost in her sorrow and despondency.
She barely recognizes the presence of anyone in the room.
She says nothing in the presence of anger, but she will talk, albeit haltingly, to someone who talks with her gently.
Mary hid her beloved daughter, Gertruda, in this house for the girl's entire life. Gertruda, now a teenager, broke out of the house a week ago and has not been seen since.
Her mother fears the worst-and is justified in doing so.
See area K42 in chapter 4 for more information on Gertruda's fate.

The malformed doll has a strange leer and wears a sackcloth dress. It belonged to Mary in her youth and was passed down to Gertruda. Gadof Blinsky, the toy maker of Vallaki (see chapter 5, area N7), made the doll.
Stitched into the hem of its dress is a frayed tag bearing the words "Is No Fun, Is No Blinsky!"

NPCs of Barovia Village
Ismark the Lesser

Ismark (LG male human veteran) is a young man who sits by himself at a corner table, sipping his wine. Other villagers call him "lsmark the Lesser" because he has lived in the shadow of his father for most of his life.
Ismark isn't a typically dour Barovian. He invites the characters to join him, offers to pay for their wine, and asks for their aid in protecting his adopted sister, Ireena

Kolyana. If they agree to help, he takes them to the bur gomaster's residence (area E4). He wants the characters to help him escort Ireena to Vallaki, a settlement in the heart of the valley, beyond the view of Castle Ravenloft and (he hopes) beyond the reach of Strahd. Ismark knows that moving Ireena is a gamble, since she's vul nerable to Strahd when she is outside their home, but he has heard assertions that Vallaki is well defended.

Ismark is as tight-lipped as any of the other Barovian villagers, unless the talk deals with Ireena or Strahd. Ismark knows everything the other villagers know. He also knows that, for some unknown reason, Strahd is attracted to Ireena and desires her above all others.

If you used the "Plea for Help" adventure hook and the characters show Ismark the letter they received, he knows the burgomaster's handwriting well enough to confirm that the letter was not written by his father.

Ismark has spent most of his adult life training with weapons in the hope of one day confronting and killing the Strahd. If the characters suggest that he accompany them, Ismark agrees provided that Ireena is first taken to a place of safety. As long as Ismark accompanies the characters, he acts as a party member for the purpose of determining each character's share of experience points (though he gains no experience points himself).
Ireena
Ireena, a striking young woman with auburn hair, has been bitten twice by Strahd.
The villagers are afraid of her and avoid her.
The characters are Ireena's best hope for protection, so she is willing to accompany them under certain conditions.
Although she appears mild, she has a strong will, and she aids the party as best she can in saving herself. She doesn't remember her early past.

She doesn't know how she came to Barovia or where she came from. Moreover, her encounters with Strahd are fuzzy memories, thanks to his vampiric charm, but she can recall clearly the blazing hunger in his eyes.
She tells the characters that wolves and other terrible creatures attacked the house night after night for weeks.
The burgomaster's heart couldn't stand the constant assault, and he died three days ago. Strangely, since his death, the house has not come under attack.
She says that no one from the village has been brave enough to help Ismark take Kolyan Indirovich to the cemetery for proper burial.
Ireena asks the characters if they would be so kind as to help Ismark deliver her father's body safely to Donavich, the local priest (area ES).
She refuses to be taken to Vallaki or anywhere else while the burgomaster lies dead on the mansion floor.
Arik the Barkeep
A pudgy little man named Arik Lorensk (CN male human commoner) tends bar.

Mindlessly, he cleans glasses, one after another. When they're all clean, he starts over. If he is spoken to, he takes orders for drinks in a dull, hollow voice. A small glass of wine costs 1 cp. A pitcher of wine costs 1 sp. After serving drinks, Arik returns to cleaning glasses. He ignores all attempts to question him.
Vistani Owners
Three Vistani spies (N female humans) named Alenka, Mirabel, and Sorvia sit at a table near the front door.
They own the tavern and see to it that all customers pay their tabs. They otherwise show little interest in the characters.
If the characters arrive in the company of other Vistani, the owners are much more likely to engage them in conversation and provide useful information.
They suggest that the characters visit Madam Eva (see chapter 2, area G) to have their fortunes read.
F. River Ivlis Crossroads
Check for a random encounter whenever the characters reach area F, unless they are accompanied by Vistani.

-An old wooden gallows creaks in a chill wind that blows down from the high ground to the west. A frayed length of rope dances from its beam. The well-worn road splits here, and a signpost opposite the gallows points off in three directions: BAROVIA VILLAGE to the east, TSER Pool to the northwest, and RAVENLOFT/VALLAKI to the southwest. The northwest fork slants down and disappears into the trees, while the southwest fork clings to an upward slope. Across from the gallows, a low wall, crumbling in places, partially encloses a small plot of graves shrouded in fog.

The Hanged One
As the characters leave the area…

-You hear a creaking noise behind you, coming from the gallows. Where there was nothing before now hangs a lifeless, gray body. The breeze turns the hanged figure slowly, so that it can fix its dead eyes upon you.

One random character sees him- or herself hanging from the gallows. The other characters see an unfamiliar Barovian. The corpse looks and smells real, and it rapidly melts away into nothing if touched or moved.
G. Tser Pool Encampment

The road gradually disappears and is replaced by a twisted, muddy path through the trees. Deep ruts in the earth are evidence of the comings and goings of wagons. The canopy of mist and branches suddenly gives way to black clouds boiling far above. There is a clearing here, next to a river that widens to form a small lake several hundred feet across. Five colorful round tents, each ten feet in diameter, are pitched outside a ring of four barrel-top wagons. A much larger tent stands near the shore of the lake, its sagging form lit from within. Near this tent, eight unbridled horses drink from the river. figures around the bonfire. A footpath continues beyond this encampment, meandering north
The mournful strains of an accordion clash with the singing of several brightly clad between the river and the forest's edge.
The eight draft horses drinking from the river are used to pull the Vistani wagons and aren't easily startled.
If the characters are brought to this camp by the Vistani, their escorts remain at the camp and don't accompany the adventuring party any farther.

Twelve Vistani (CN male and female human bandits) are standing and sitting around the fire, telling stories and guzzling wine. They are intoxicated and have disadvantage on attack rolls and ability checks. Three sober Vistani (CN male and female human bandit captains) are resting in three of the four wagons but leap quickly into action if an alarm is raised.

Although the Vistani in this camp are in league with Strahd, they attack only if the characters provoke them with threats or insults. Otherwise, the characters are offered flasks of wine and invited to join the reverie.

If the characters linger at the camp, continue with "A Vistana's Tale" below. If they seem in a hurry to leave, one of the Vistani tells them, "It was fated that you would visit our humble camp. Madam Eva foretold your coming. She awaits you." The Vistana then points to the largest tent. If the characters head that way, continue with "Madam Eva's Tent."

The Vistana storyteller doesn't remember the wizard's name, but recalls that it sounded do so.

Madam Eva's Tent
If the characters decide to see Madam Eva, read:

Magic flames cast a reddish glow over the interior of this tent, revealing a low table covered in a black velvet cloth. Glints of light seem to flash from a crystal ball on the table as a hunched figure peers into its depths. As the crone speaks, her voice crackles like dry weeds. "At last you have arrived!" Cackling laughter bursts like mad lightning from her withered lips.
Madam Eva is waiting for the party at her caravan.

She states that she has been expecting their arrival, though she expected extras: a harengon, a satyr, a halfling, perhaps others that exist but may not have joined yet. She pauses and then states that the Satyr and the Harengon are following, after all.
Madam Eva (see appendix D) speaks the name of each party member and makes some reference to that individual's past deeds.
Dusty, one who bounds mischief, you approach me with both mysticism and fear.
Elincia, you who walks with death, do you feel accomplished with your travels?
Lysandra, you too have traveled far, but you will never be able to escape that which haunts you.
Haus, you have not traveled far, nor have you grown from your mistakes. When will you move past what has happened to your people?
Friday, oh Friday, fear not the loss of your friends, but the loss of yourself.
Ismark and Ireena, haunted by Strahd himself, I fear your end will not be the happy ever after you seek.

She then asks the characters if they want their fortunes read. If they say yes, Madam Eva produces a worn deck of cards and proceeds with the sequence outlined in chapter 1. (If the characters don't want a reading of their fates, continue playing using the card reading you performed before starting the adventure.)
Madam Eva might seem mad, but she is, in fact, cunning and sharp of mind.
She has met a good many adventurers in her time and knows they can't be fully trusted.
She wants to free the land of Barovia from its curse, and her fate is interwoven with Strahd's (see appendix D for details).
She does the vampire's bidding when called upon and does nothing to anger Strahd or bring harm to the Vistani.
She never gives aid and never asks for any.
Treasure
Caravan's NPCs:
Madam Eva
-Mysterious fortune teller with a weathered face

-Offers a Tarokka Card reading, revealing cryptic hints and locations related to the items needed to seal Strahd away. Madam Eva speaks of destiny and the party's role in shaping the fate of this land.
Vassi von Holtz

-Dashing and enigmatic, Vasili von Holtz is a charismatic nobleman who frequently travels with Madam Eva's caravan. He exudes an aura of mystery and charm that captivates those who meet him. Vasili is a skilled musician and often entertains the campfire gatherings with hauntingly beautiful melodies played on his violin.

-Vasili maintains a suave and friendly demeanor, making him a favorite among the caravan's patrons. He has a way of drawing secrets and stories out of people, which he occasionally shares during the Tarokka Card readings, providing valuable insights and hints about the challenges that lie ahead.

-However, beneath his charming exterior, Vasili holds his own secrets and is not what he appears to be. His true identity is a carefully guarded mystery, and his motives remain concealed. Vasili's presence in the caravan adds an element of intrigue and complexity to the party's interactions with Madam Eva and the world of Barovia.
H. Tser Falls
If the characters reach area H by following the footpath from the Vistani encampment (area G), read:

-You follow the river to the base of a canyon, at the far end of which a great waterfall spills into a pool, billowing forth clouds of cold mist. A great stone bridge spans the canyon nearly one thousand feet overhead.
If the characters are on the high road instead:

-You follow the dirt road as it clings to the side of a mountain and ends before an arching bridge of mold encrusted stone that spans a natural chasm. Gargoyles cloaked in black moss perch on the corners of the bridge, their frowns weatherworn. On the mountainous side of the bridge, a waterfall spills into a misty pool nearly a thousand feet below. The pool feeds a river that meanders into the fog-shrouded pines that blanket the valley.
Valley Details
The chasm's walls are slippery and sheer, and can't be scaled without the aid of magic or a climber's kit.
The bridge is slick with moisture but safe to cross.
The road south of the bridge leads down the mountain side to area F; the road north cuts through the moun tains to area I.
The gargoyles on the bridge are harmless sculptures.
I. Black Carriage

Even here, in the mountains, the forest and the fog are inescapable. Ahead, the dirt road splits in two, widening toward the east. There you see patches of cobblestone, suggesting that the eastern branch was once an import ant thoroughfare.
If Strahd has invited the characters to Castle Ravenloft or otherwise wants to steer them in his direction, add:

-Parked at the fork in the road, pointed east, is a large black carriage drawn by two black horses. The horses snort puffs of steamy breath into the chill mountain air. The side door of the carriage swings open silently.
Extra:
The two black draft horses are under Strahd's control. The horses wait for the characters to pile into the car riage if they so desire. There is room inside for eight
of them. If they get into the carriage, the horses draw
it down the road to area]. The horses can't be discour aged from their course, not even by a skilled teamster. Characters who don't want to travel east in the car

riage can follow the road northwest through a set of iron gates (area B) that open as they approach and close be hind them, or the characters can travel south along the winding road to the bridge at Tser Falls (area H).
J. Gates of Ravenloft
The following text assumes that the characters arrive here in the carriage from area I. Modify the text as needed if the characters arrive by another means.

After winding through the forest and craggy mountain peaks, the road takes a sudden turn to the east, and the startling, awesome presence of Castle Ravenloft towers before you. The carriage comes to a dead stop before twin turrets of stone, broken from years of exposure. Beyond these guard towers is the precipice of a fifty-foot-wide, fog-filled chasm that disappears into unknown depths.

A lowered drawbridge of old, shored-up wooden beams stretches across the chasm, between you and the archway to the courtyard. The chains of the drawbridge creak in the wind, their rust-eaten iron straining under the weight. From atop the high walls, stone

gargoyles stare at you out of their hollow eye sockets and grin hideously. A rotting wooden portcullis, green with growth, hangs above the entry tunnel. Beyond this location, the main doors of Ravenloft stand open. A rich, warm light spills from within, flooding the courtyard. Torches flutter sadly in sconces on both sides of the open doors.

The drawbridge appears sturdy, but a few of its boards are missing and it creaks and groans under any weight. Each time a creature other than Strahd or a horse that draws his carriage crosses the drawbridge, there is a 1d100 (5 percent) chance of one of its boards breaking under the creature. If a board breaks, the creature must succeed on a DC 10 Dexterity saving throw or fall to the bottom of the cliffs, 1,000 feet below. If a companion is within 5 feet of the creature and reaches out to grab it, the creature has advantage on the save.
Green Slime

A patch of  green slime (see "Dungeon Hazards" in chapter 5 of the Dungeon Master's Guide) clings to the portcullis in the entry tunnel, and can be spotted with a successful DC 20 Wisdom (Perception) check. The slime will not fall on characters entering the castle, but it does fall on the first character who leaves by this route.
K. Castle Ravenloft
L. Lake Zarovich
At the foot of a mountain, nestled in the misty forest, is a large lake. The water is perfectly still and dark, reflecting the black clouds overhead like a monstrous mirror.

If the characters arrive along the shore north of Vallaki in the daytime, add: Pulled up along the south shore are three small rowboats. A fourth boat can be seen in the middle of the lake, with a lone figure sitting in it, fishing pole in hand.

Each rowboat can safely hold five people. The person fishing on the lake is Bluto Krogarov (NE male human commoner), a resident of Vallaki. He is in a trance and doesn't respond to anything or anyone unless attacked. His boat is 400 feet from the nearest shore. Tied up in the boat is a seven-year-old Vistana named Arabelle (LN female human commoner with 2 hit points and no effective attacks). She is bound with hempen rope, wrapped in a burlap sack, and lying  prone so that she can't be seen or heard from the shore.
Roleplaying Bluto
Bluto Krogarov is a destitute drunkard. He's desperate to catch some fish and trade them for wine at the Blue Water Inn. After he was unable to catch a single fish for a week, he
kidnapped Arabelle, believing that Vistani are lucky. He intends to sacrifice her to the lake, hoping it will give up some of its fish in return.

If the characters watch Bluto from the shore for several minutes, or if they row out into the lake to greet him, he tosses the burlap sack into the water, watches it sink, and waits with fishing pole in hand for his reward.
Bluto is a hollow shell of a man, barely able to understand his own actions. He is unarmed and does nothing to aid or thwart the characters.
Roleplaying Arabelle

Characters who act quickly can save Arabelle before she drowns. A character on the shore must succeed on a DC 15 Strength (Athletics) check to reach her in time. The DC is 10 for characters who took a rowboat out onto the lake.

Arabelle has alabaster-white skin and raven-black hair. If rescued, she demands to be returned to her family's camp outside Vallaki (chapter 5, area N9). She is certain that her father, Luvash, will give the characters a reward for doing so.

A descendant of Madam Eva with the blood of Barovian royalty in her veins, Arabelle is unaware of her connection to Strahd. She acts more like an adult than a child. Despite her recent misadventure, she believes that a great destiny awaits her.
M. Mad Mage of Mount Baratok

This encounter can occur anywhere along the base of Mount Baratok. North of the mountain lake, the trees begin their steady climb up the slopes of Mount Baratok, its monolithic presence oppressive at this distance. The ground here is rocky, uneven, and tiring to navigate. Even the wolves avoid this neck of the woods. Soon, you climb above the blanket of fog that engulfs the valley. Dark thunderclouds roll overhead.

You see an elk standing on a rocky spur about sixty feet away. Suddenly, it assumes the form of a man in tattered black robes. His hair and beard are long, black, and streaked with gray, and his eyes crackle with eldritch power.

The Mad Mage of Mount Baratok (CN male human archmage) came to Barovia more than a year ago to free its people from Strahd's tyranny, but he underestimated Strahd's hold over the land and the creatures in it.
After a battle between the two in Castle Ravenloft, Strahd drove the Mad Mage to the mountains and sent the wizard hurling over Tser Falls (area H).

The wizard, his staff and spellbook lost, survived the fall and retreated into the mountains, hoping to regain his power, only to be driven mad by the realization that he no longer has any hope of defeating Strahd or freeing the people of the vampire's damned realm.
The Mad Mage has forgotten his name and the world whence he came. In fact, he doesn't remember anything that happened before the madness.
He suffers from the paranoia that powerful enemies are hunting him, and that their evil agents are everywhere and watching him.

Believing that the characters aim to kill him, the Mad Mage unleashes his destructive magic. As he tears into them, he shouts, "You think my magic has grown weak? Think again!" If he is reduced to 50 hit points or fewer, he shouts, "Tell your dark masters they can break my body, but never my spirit!" He then tries to escape.

Under normal circumstances, a greater restoration spell cast on the Mad Mage would restore his wits and ends the madness, allowing him to remember that he is none other than Mordenkainen, an archmage of Oerth and the leader of a powerful group of adventurers called the Circle of Eight. But in this case, the Mad Mage has cast a mind blank spell on himself.

As long as that spell remains in effect, his sanity can't be restored by any spell. If the characters surmise that powerful magic is preventing them from restoring the Mad Mage's wits, they can, with a successful DC 15 Charisma (Persuasion) check, convince the Mad Mage to divulge the reason why their spell failed.

A character can also ascertain the cause of the spell's failure with a successful DC 18 Intelligence (Arcana) check. The Mad Mage's mind blank spell has a remaining duration of 3d6 hours, after which his madness can be cured normally.

The Mad Mage has a different spell list from that of the archmage in the Monster Manual, and he has already used one 1st-level spell slot to cast mage armor on himself, one 4th-level spell slot to cast polymorph on himself, one 7th-level spell slot to cast Mordenkainen's magnificent mansion (see "The Mad Mage's Mansion"), and one 8th-level spell slot to cast mind blank on himself.

Cantrips (at will): fire bolt, light, mage hand, prestidigitation, shocking grasp 1st level (4 slots): detect magic, mage armor, magic missile, shield 2nd level (3 slots): mirror image, misty step, web 3rd level (3 slots): counterspell, fly, lightning bolt 4th level (3 slots): Mordenkainen's faithful hound, polymorph, stoneskin 5th level (3 slots): Bigby's hand, cone of cold, scrying 6th level (1 slot): true seeing
7th level (1 slot): Mordenkainen's magnificent mansion
The Mad Mage's Mansion

If the characters rescue the archmage from his madness, he invites them to his "mansion." He leads them up the mountain to an invisible doorway that serves as the entrance to his extradimensional lair, created using the Mordenkainen's magnificent mansion spell. There, he provides them with food and sanctuary away from the prying eyes of Strahd and his spies. Characters are free to take a short or long rest, during which time they aren't disturbed.

Mordenkainen is familiar with worlds beyond his own. For example, if the characters come from the Forgotten Realms and mention this fact to Mordenkainen, he asks them if they know his old friend Elminster of Shadowdale.

If Mordenkainen isn't the party's ally as foretold in Madam Eva's card reading (see chapter 1), he declines to join them if asked. With his wits restored, he sets out to find his missing staff and spellbook, leaving the characters on their own. He doesn't allow them to help him, for he fears they might be tempted to steal either his staff or his spellbook. (Being an adventurer himself, he knows how the lure of powerful magic can bring out the worst in adventurers.) Before he leaves, as a parting gift, the archmage imbues each character with a charm of heroism (see "Supernatural Gifts" in chapter 7 of the Dungeon Master's Guide).

With his sanity restored, Mordenkainen can be stubborn and difficult even with his friends, and doesn't suffer fools. He normally spends more time listening than talking, but when he does speak, his pronouncements are authoritative and not to be questioned.

The archmage has never had his fortune read by Madam Eva and doesn't care to, but if he is told about the Holy Symbol of Ravenkind, the Tome of Strahd, and the Sunsword, he insists that these items be recovered before he and the party confront Strahd. If Strahd is defeated and Mordenkainen survives, the archmage gladly accompanies the characters back to their world if they invite him, if only not to disappoint them.
N. Town of Vallaki
LOCATED CLOSE TO THE SHORES OF LAKE Zarovich, the town of Vallaki seems like a safe haven against the evils of the Svalich Woods, if not Strahd himself.
The town lies beyond the sight of Castle Ravenloft and doesn't, at first blush, seem as de pressed (or oppressed) as the village of Barovia farther east.
Characters who spend time inVallaki, however, quickly realize that there is no happiness here, only false hope-which Strahd himself cultivates.
8th level (1 slot): mind blank 9th level (1 slot): time stop
Vallaki was founded not long after Strahd's armies conquered the valley by an ancestor of the town's current burgomaster, Baron Vargas Vallakovich.
The Val lakoviches have royal blood in their veins and have long believed themselves superior to the Zarovich line.
Baron Vallakovich has deluded himself into believing that hope and happiness are the keys to Vallaki's salvation.
If he can make everyone in Vallaki happy, the burgomaster thinks that the town will somehow escape Strahd's grasp and return to the forgotten world whence it came.

He stages one festival after another to bolster the spirits of the townsfolk, but most Vallakians consider these festivals to be pointless, meaningless affairs more likely to incur Strahd's wrath than to provide any hope for the future.
In the last festival, Baron Vallakovich had townsfolk parade through the streets with the severed heads of wolves on pikes.
His next event, which the burgomaster has dubbed the Festival of the Blazing Sun, is soon to get under way (see the "Special Events" section at the end of this chapter).
Weatherworn garlands from previous festivals still hang from the eaves ofVallaki's buildings, and work has begun on a large wicker sun, to be set ablaze in the town square on the day of the festival.
In the days leading up to the festival, Baron Vallakovich has begun arresting local malcontents and throwing them in the stocks so that his efforts aren't ruined by "those of little hope or faith."
Approaching the Town

The Old Svalich Road meanders into a valley watched over by dark, brooding mountains to the north and south. The woods recede, revealing a sullen mountain burg surrounded by a wooden palisade. Thick fog presses up against this wall, as though looking for a way inside, hoping to catch the town aslumber. The dirt road ends at a set of sturdy iron gates with a pair of shadowy figures standing behind them. Planted in the ground and flanking the road outside the gates are a half-dozen
pikes with wolves' heads impaled on them.

A 15-foot-high wall encloses the town, its vertical logs held together with thick ropes and mortar. The top of each log has been sharpened to a point. Wooden scaf folding hugs the inside of the palisade twelve feet off the ground, enabling guards to peer over the wall there.
Town Gates
Three tall gates made of iron bars lead into town:
The north gate is sometimes called the Zarovich Gate, or "the gate to the lake," because it leads to Lake Zarovich (chapter 2, area L).
The west gate is referred to as the Sunset Gate, even though no living person in Vallaki has seen an un dimmed sunset. A few abandoned cottages line the road outside this gate.
The east gate, also known as the Morning Gate, or, as some locals like to call it, the Mourning Gate.
General info:
Heavy iron chains with iron padlocks keep the gates shut at night. During the day, the gates are closed but not typically locked.
Two town guards (LG male and female humans) stand just inside each gate.
Instead of spears, they carry pikes (reach 10 ft., ldlO + 1 piercing damage on a hit).
■ These weapons are long enough to stab creatures through the bars of the gate.
The guards greet all visitors with suspicion, particularly those who arrive at night.
If the characters arrive at night, one or more of them must succeed on a DC 20 Charisma (Persuasion) check to convince the guards to unlock the gate and let them enter.
If trouble breaks out at one of the gates, the guards there cry out, "To arms!" Their shouts are echoed across Vallaki, putting the entire town on alert within minutes.
Vallaki has twenty-four human guards, half of whom are on duty at any given time (six stand watch at the gates, six patrol the walls).
The town can also muster a militia of fifty able-bodied human commoners armed with clubs, daggers, and torches.
House Occupants
If the characters explore a residence other than the burgomaster's mansion (area N3), roll a d20 and consult the following table to determine the house's occupant.
RATS
A house infested with rats appears abandoned at first. The rats are servants of Strahd and attack if the charac ters explore the interior of the house.
TOWNSFOLK

A house ofVallakian townsfolk contains ld4 adults (LG male and female human commoners)and ldB - 1 chil dren (LG male and female human noncombatants). Any one who listens at the door hears chatter from within.
Townsfolk won't willingly invite strangers into their homes, but they will speak with characters from behind closed doors or while standing in their vestibules.
CULTISTS

A cult haven contains 2d4 Vallakian adults (LE male and female cultists) and one cult fanatic (LE male or female) who leads them in prayer or orchestrates ritual sacrifices. These cultists worship devils and consider Lady Fiona Wachter  (see area N4) to be their spiritual leader.
Vallaki Lore
In addition to the information known to all Barovians(see: Barovian Lore in chapter 2), Vallakians know the following bits of local lore:
The Blue Water Inn (area N2) offered food, wine, and shelter to visitors. A stranger with pointed ears is staying there. He came to Barovia from a distant land, riding into town on a carnival wagon.
The burgomaster, Baron Vargas Vallakovich, has decreed that the Festival of the Blazing Sun will be held in the town square (area NB) in three days.
The previous festival, which he called the Wolf's Head Jam boree, was less than a week ago.
Vallaki has endured at least one festival every week for the past several years.
Some Valakians believe that the festivals keep the devil Strahd at bay.
Others think they provide no protection or benefit whatsoever.
Most consider them dismal affairs.
Those who speak ill of the festivals are declared by the burgomaster to be in league with the devil Strahd and arrested.
Some are thrown in the stocks (area NB), while others are taken to the burgomaster's mansion so that the baron can purge them of their evil.
The burgomaster's henchman, Izek Strazni, has a history of violence as well as a fiendish deformity: a monstrous arm with which he can conjure fire.
Fear of Izek keeps the baron's enemies at bay.

No one hates the burgomaster more than Lady Fiona Wachter, who is often quoted as saying, "I'd rather serve the devil than a madman." She owns an old house in town (area N4) but rarely leaves her estate.
Her two adult sons, Nikolai and Karl, are local trouble makers.
Lady Wachter also has a mad daughter whom she keeps locked away.
The burgomaster doesn't confront Fiona or her offspring because he is afraid of Lady Wachter, whose family has old ties to Strahd.
Purple flashes of light have been seen emanating from the attic of the burgomaster's mansion.
Wolves and direwolves prowl the woods and aren't afraid to attack travelers on the Old Svalich Road.
Well-armed groups of hunters and trappers have managed to kill several of the wolves, but more keep coming.

It's too dangerous to go fishing on Lake Zarovich (chapter 2, area L), but the threat of Strahd's wolves hasn't stopped Bluto Krogarov, the town drunk, from trying. He sets out each morning and returns every evening, but hasn't caught any fish in a while.

There have been no recent sightings of the Mad Mage of Mount Baratok (chapter 2, area M). Folks used to see him skulking along the north shore of Lake Za rovich, shooting lightning bolts into the water to kill the fish. (If the characters seem interested in a meeting. this wizard, locals recommend that they use the fishing boats on the south shore to cross the lake, because it's shorter and a lot less dangerous than walking around the lake.)
There's a Vistani camp in the woods southwest of town (area N9). The Vistani there aren't very friendly. Vistani aren't welcome in Vallaki.
West of town is a village that has been abandoned for decades. It's burgomaster committed some terrible offense and incurred the wrath of strahd.
Sublocations
N1. St. Andral's Church

This slouching, centuries-old stone church has a bulging steeple in the back and walls lined with cracked, stained glass windows depicting pious saints. A fence of wrought iron encloses a garden of gravestones next to the church. A thin mist creeps among the graves.
This church is dedicated to the Morninglord and named after St. Andral, whose bones once rested under the altar (see the "Bones of St. Andral" section).

Father Lucian Petrovich (LG male human priest) oversees the church and does his best to raise spirits. Assisting him is an orphan and altar boy named Yeska (LG male human noncombatant). A brawny lad with a perpetually furrowed brow named Milivoj (see below) tends the grounds and digs graves.

At night, the church is packed with 2d6 + 6  frightened Vallakian adults (male and female human commoners) and 2d6 equally terrified Vallakian children (male and female human noncombatants). Father Lucian offers his nightly congregation his prayers and the promise of St. Andral's protection. Among Father Lucian's nightly flock is a sad old woman named Willemina Rikalova . Her son, the shoemaker Udo Lukovich , has been imprisoned for speaking out against the burgomaster (see area N3m). She prays that her son will be set free.
Bones of St. Andral

Until recently, the church was protected from Strahd's depredations by the bones of St. Andral, which were sealed in a crypt beneath the church's main altar. But now the church is at risk because someone broke into the crypt a few nights ago and stole the bones. Until recently, Father Lucian was the only person in Vallaki who knew about the bones, but he recalls mentioning them to Yeska over a month ago to put the fearful boy at ease. After the bones were stolen, Father Lucian asked Yeska if he told anyone else about the bones. The boy nodded but wouldn't divulge a name.

The culprit is Milivoj, whom Father Lucian correctly suspects. But the priest has been reluctant to confront Milivoj because the lad is so temperamental. Father Lucian has not reported the theft for fear of the distress that the news might cause, and he doesn't want to ruin the burgomaster's festival. If the party includes a good-aligned cleric or paladin, Father Lucian mentions the theft in the hope that the characters can provide assistance.

St. Andral's crypt is a 10-foot-square, 5-foot high chamber beneath the chapel. To reach the crypt, Milivoj used his shovel to pry up the chapel floorboards. (The boards have since been replaced.) If one of the characters confronts Milivoj and succeeds on a DC 10 Charisma (Intimidation) check, he admits that Yeska told him about the bones. He also admits to passing along the information to Henrik van der Voort, the local coffin maker (area N6), and to stealing the bones for Henrik in return for money to help feed his younger sisters and brothers.
The theft of the bones has left the church vulnerable to attack by Strahd's minions (see "St. Andral's Feast" in the "Special Events" section at the end of this chapter). If the bones are
returned to their resting place, St. Andral's church once again becomes hallowed ground, as though the building was protected by a hallow spell.
N2. Blue Water Inn

Gray smoke issues from the chimney of this large, two-story wooden building with a stone foundation and sagging tile roof, upon which several ravens have perched. A painted wooden sign hanging above the main entrance depicts a blue waterfall.
The Blue Water Inn is Vallaki's main gathering place for locals, especially at night. The innkeeper, Urwin Martikov , considers the inn a sanctuary from the evils of this land.
In the event of trouble, the windows and doors can all be barred shut from within.
A bed for the night costs 1 ep.
Characters looking for something to eat are fed hot beet soup and fresh bread at no additional charge.
A cooked wolf steak costs 1 ep.
The inn offers a pint of Purple Grapemash No. 3 wine for 3 cp, or a pint of the superior Red Dragon Crush wine for 1 sp.
Urwin is hurt if the characters complain about the wines, for his family makes them.
The inn's wine supply is almost depleted, and the latest delivery from the Wizard of Wines winery is overdue.

If the characters claim to be adventurers, Urwin asks them if they would be so kind as to find out what's holding up the latest shipment, promising them free room and board if they return with the wine.
Keepers of the Feather

Urwin Martikov (LG male human) is a wereraven (see appendix D) and a high-ranking member of the Keepers of the Feather, a secret society of wereravens that opposes Strahd. Urwin's wife and business partner, Danika Dorakova (LG female human), is also a wereraven, as are their two sons, Brom and Bray. The boys have only 7 hit points each and, at ages eleven and nine, are too young to be effective combatants.

At any given time, another 1d4 wereravens (members of the Keepers of the Feather) are present at the inn, either perched on the rooftop in raven form or huddled inside in human form. These wereravens are loyal friends of the Martikovs and help protect the inn.

If the characters earn the trust of the wereravens in Vallaki, the Keepers of the Feather will watch their backs. The next time the characters get themselves in serious trouble, you can have a group of 1d4 wereravens show up to rescue or otherwise help them.
Fortunes of Ravenloft

If your card reading reveals that a treasure is hidden at the inn, the Keepers of the Feather don't reveal where the treasure is until they know the characters are capable of protecting it. As a way of testing their abilities, Urwin gives the characters the following quest:

Urwin takes you aside and keeps his voice low. "My supply of wine is nearly gone, and the next shipment is overdue. I'll give you what you seek if you bring me my wine. The vineyard and winery is a few miles west of here. Just follow the Old Svalich Road and the signs."

Urwin fails to mention that his cantankerous father, Davian Martikov, owns the local winery and vineyard, the Wizard of Wines (chapter 12). There's bad blood between Urwin and his father (whom Urwin and Danika refer to as "the old crow"). Although Urwin could easily visit the winery himself, he considers dealing with his father to be a worthy test of the characters' competence, and he makes good on his promise if they complete the quest and return with his wine shipment.
